options: A. The 54.8 °C reduction in T1 means thermal runaway could be initiated within the normal operating temperature range of a battery thermal management system.
B. Because T2 is nearly unchanged, the overall thermal safety of the cell is effectively preserved after a single overcharge to 120% SOC.

C. The large drop in T1 is primarily caused by lithium plating and SEI decomposition at the anode, which introduce highly reactive metallic lithium that participates in side reactions at lower temperatures.
D. The unchanged T2 is explained by the separator melting temperature remaining constant regardless of internal gas generation from overcharge.
E. Capacity retention after a single overcharge to 120% SOC confirms that electrochemical and thermal degradation are coupled and progress at the same rate.
F. Loss of active material begins immediately upon any degree of overcharge above 100% SOC, contributing to the T1 reduction.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: T1 of 41 °C falls within normal thermal management operating range, so thermal runaway may be triggered under normal conditions.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: T2 stability does not preserve overall safety; severely reduced T1 means self-heating begins much earlier, increasing runaway risk.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Plated metallic lithium is more reactive than intercalated lithium and reacts with electrolyte at lower temperatures, lowering T1.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Separator melting temperature is constant; internal gas generation from overcharge does not alter it, so T2 is largely unaffected.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows capacity is little affected while thermal stability is severely degraded, demonstrating the two metrics decouple after overcharge.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Loss of active material only begins between 105–110% SOC, not immediately above 100% SOC; this overgeneralizes the threshold condition.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing which degradation metrics change after overcharge and identifying the correct mechanism for T1 reduction vs. T2 stability
analysis.primary_boundary: T1 drops severely while T2 is nearly unchanged; capacity is preserved but thermal stability is not
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring overall thermal safety from T2 stability alone
Assuming LAM begins at any SOC above 100%

solver_view.stem: During accelerated rate calorimetry on overcharged lithium-ion cells, the interval time Δt between sudden voltage drop and thermal runaway triggering increases from 2656.6 s at 105% SOC to 9215.1 s at 120% SOC. Simultaneously, the temperature at which the voltage drop occurs (TV) is 31 °C lower at 120% SOC than at 105% SOC. Which of the following correctly identifies the WRONG interpretation of these two observations?
options: A. The longer Δt at higher overcharge means the cell has more time between voltage drop and thermal runaway, suggesting that greater overcharge degree paradoxically extends the pre-runaway warning window.
B. The lower TV at higher overcharge indicates that internal short-circuit or CID-like events are triggered at a lower temperature when overcharge is more severe.
C. Because Δt increases with overcharge degree, higher overcharge inherently improves the cell's resistance to thermal runaway once voltage drop occurs.
D. The combination of lower TV and longer Δt at 120% SOC implies the cell enters a degraded electrochemical state earlier but takes longer to accumulate sufficient heat for thermal runaway.
E. The increase in Δt from 105% to 120% SOC is approximately 3.5-fold, consistent with the reported data, and reflects a change in the thermal progression pathway after voltage drop.

option_judgments.label: Correct (wrong interpretation as requested)
option_judgments.justification: This is actually a factually accurate observation about the data; it is NOT the wrong interpretation—it correctly notes the paradox. Wait—re-evaluating: A states the paradox accurately and is a valid reading of the data.

option_judgments.label: Correct interpretation (not wrong)
option_judgments.justification: Lower TV at higher overcharge is directly supported; voltage drop occurs at lower temperature with greater overcharge severity.

option_judgments.label: Correct answer (wrong interpretation)
option_judgments.justification: Longer Δt reflects altered thermal progression, not improved runaway resistance; T1 is severely reduced, so overall safety is worse, not better.

option_judgments.label: Correct interpretation (not wrong)
option_judgments.justification: Lower TV combined with longer Δt is consistent with earlier electrochemical failure but slower heat accumulation to runaway threshold.

option_judgments.label: Correct interpretation (not wrong)
option_judgments.justification: The ~3.5-fold increase in Δt is explicitly reported and reflects a change in thermal progression after voltage drop.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Identifying a causal overclaim that misreads longer pre-runaway interval as improved safety despite worsened T1
analysis.primary_boundary: Longer Δt does not imply improved thermal runaway resistance; T1 reduction dominates overall safety assessment
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring improved runaway resistance from increased Δt alone without considering T1 reduction

solver_view.stem: A diagnostic engineer uses the dV/dt relaxation peak arrival time to monitor overcharge-induced degradation in lithium-ion cells. Cells are overcharged to 105%, 110%, 115%, and 120% SOC. Gas analysis of a cell at 120% SOC shows CO2 at 62.23%, with CO, CH4, and C2H6 also present. Cathode morphology of secondary particles is intact after slight overcharge. Which two statements are supported by the evidence?
options: A. The dV/dt peak arrival time decreases linearly with overcharge degree, allowing it to be used as a quantitative proxy for reversible lithium content loss.
B. CO2 dominance in the gas phase indicates that cathode structural collapse is the primary source of gas generation during slight overcharge.
C. Slight overcharge causes electrolyte and CEI film decomposition at the cathode without destroying secondary particle morphology.
D. Because cathode morphology is preserved, the cathode contributes no degradation to capacity fade or thermal instability after slight overcharge.
E. Loss of active material and conductivity loss both begin simultaneously at the onset of any overcharge above 100% SOC.
F. The linear relationship between dV/dt peak time and overcharge degree implies that capacity fade also decreases linearly with overcharge degree.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: An excellent linear relationship between dV/dt peak arrival time and overcharge degree is explicitly reported, linked to reversible lithium content.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: CO2 dominance is attributed to electrolyte and CEI decomposition, not cathode structural collapse, which is explicitly ruled out.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Slight overcharge causes electrolyte and CEI film decomposition without destroying cathode secondary particle morphology.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Preserved morphology does not mean zero cathode contribution; CEI and electrolyte decomposition at the cathode still occur and affect cell behavior.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: LAM begins only between 105–110% SOC; conductivity loss and LLI begin earlier, so they do not start simultaneously.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The linear relationship applies to reversible lithium content, not capacity fade; single overcharge has little influence on capacity.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing valid diagnostic inferences from mechanism substitution and boundary overreach errors in overcharge degradation analysis
analysis.primary_boundary: Linear dV/dt trend applies to reversible lithium, not capacity; cathode morphology intact but CEI/electrolyte still decomposes
disallowed_extrapolations: Attributing CO2 to cathode structural collapse
Inferring capacity linearity from lithium content linearity

solver_view.prompt: A lithium-ion cell is overcharged once to 120% SOC. Post-overcharge measurements show: self-heating onset temperature T1 = 41 °C (down from 95.8 °C for a fresh cell), thermal runaway triggering temperature T2 ≈ 161 °C (nearly unchanged from 163 °C fresh), and capacity is largely preserved. Explain mechanistically why T1 decreases so sharply while T2 remains nearly constant, and describe the safety implication of the T1 value falling to 41 °C for a cell returned to normal 100% SOC operation.

ground_truth.gold_answer: T1 decreases sharply because overcharge causes lithium plating at the anode and SEI film decomposition. Plated metallic lithium is more reactive than intercalated lithium and initiates exothermic side reactions with the electrolyte at much lower temperatures, lowering the self-heating onset. T2 remains nearly constant because it is governed by separator melting temperature, which is a material property unaffected by internal gas generation from overcharge. The safety implication is critical: at 41 °C, T1 falls within the normal operating temperature range of a battery thermal management system. This means that even during routine operation at 100% SOC after a single overcharge event, the cell may spontaneously enter self-heating and trigger thermal runaway without any additional abuse, despite appearing electrochemically normal based on capacity alone.
must_include: lithium plating and SEI decomposition as the mechanism for T1 reduction
plated lithium reacts at lower temperatures than intercalated lithium
separator melting temperature governs T2 and is unaffected by gas generation
T1 of 41 °C falls within normal thermal management operating range
thermal runaway risk under normal operating conditions despite capacity preservation
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming cathode structural collapse contributes to T1 reduction
Claiming T2 changes are negligible because overcharge degree is low
Inferring that capacity preservation means thermal safety is also preserved
Generalizing T1 reduction mechanism to all overcharge levels without noting the threshold dependence of LAM

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-step causal reasoning linking overcharge mechanism to differential effects on T1 vs. T2, and translating T1 value into a system-level safety implication
analysis.primary_boundary: T1 is governed by anode reactivity (lithium plating); T2 is governed by separator melting temperature; these are independent mechanisms
intended_error_modes: mechanism substitution (attributing T1 drop to cathode)
causal overclaim (T2 stability implies overall safety)
scale transfer overclaim (capacity preservation implies thermal safety)

solver_view.stem: An NCM523 pouch battery is subjected to high-temperature shock from a hydrogen flame (1100–1400 °C). Engineers monitor the surface temperature rise rate to identify thermal runaway stages. The onset of self-heating (T1) is defined by a rate threshold of 8 °C/s, and the onset of ignition (T2) by 30 °C/s. A simulation model predicts T2 with 16.5% error but predicts peak surface temperature T3 with 0.68% error. Which TWO conclusions are directly supported by these conditions?
options: A. The simulation model is more reliable for predicting the maximum surface temperature than for predicting the ignition onset temperature.
B. The 16.5% error at T2 and 0.68% error at T3 imply the model underestimates heat release at all intermediate stages.
C. T1 and T2 are distinguished solely by the magnitude of the temperature rise rate threshold, not by different physical mechanisms.
D. The T2 threshold of 30 °C/s is more than three times the T1 threshold of 8 °C/s, so a battery passing T1 has not necessarily reached ignition onset.
E. Because T3 prediction error is below 1%, the model can be directly transferred to LFP chemistry without re-parameterization.
F. The higher prediction error at T2 than T3 suggests the transition from self-heating to ignition is harder to capture accurately than the peak thermal response.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 0.68% error at T3 vs 16.5% at T2 directly supports higher reliability for peak temperature prediction.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Intermediate stage accuracy is not reported; inferring systematic underestimation across all stages is an unsupported extrapolation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The definitions specify different rate thresholds; attributing them to identical physical mechanisms contradicts the staged causal sequence implied by separate definitions.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 30 °C/s > 8 °C/s; exceeding T1 threshold does not satisfy T2 threshold, so self-heating onset does not imply ignition onset.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states other chemistries require re-parameterization; low T3 error for NCM523 does not license direct transfer to LFP.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Higher error at the ignition transition (T2) vs near-zero error at peak (T3) is consistent with the ignition onset being harder to model accurately.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Interpreting differential model prediction accuracy across thermal runaway stages and applying threshold definitions to stage discrimination
analysis.primary_boundary: Model accuracy claims are specific to NCM523 pouch cells; T1/T2 thresholds are definitional, not universal
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing intermediate-stage error from endpoint errors
Transferring NCM523 model accuracy to LFP without re-parameterization

solver_view.stem: LFP and NCM523 pouch batteries are tested under identical high-temperature shock conditions. LFP shows a higher onset temperature of thermal runaway, a lower peak temperature, and a post-combustion mass loss rate of 0.31% versus 34.3% for NCM523. The thermal stability of LFP is attributed to its olivine structure with strong covalent P-O bonds, which inhibits oxygen release. NCM523 cathode–electrolyte reactions initiate above 180 °C; LFP cathode–electrolyte reactions initiate above 240 °C. Which statements are INCORRECT?
options: A. The lower mass loss of LFP under high-temperature shock is consistent with its inhibited oxygen release mechanism.
B. Because LFP has a higher onset temperature and lower peak temperature than NCM523, LFP batteries cannot undergo thermal runaway under any practical abuse condition.
C. The 60 °C gap between NCM523 and LFP cathode–electrolyte reaction onset temperatures is a direct consequence of the difference in their crystal structures.
D. NCM523 batteries will always ignite before LFP batteries regardless of the specific abuse mode applied, because their cathode reaction onset is lower.
E. The olivine P-O bond strength contributes to LFP's thermal advantage by requiring higher energy input to release oxygen compared to layered oxide cathodes.
F. The mass loss difference between LFP and NCM523 after combustion could be partly explained by the greater oxygen availability from NCM523 cathode decomposition.

option_judgments.label: Correct (not incorrect)
option_judgments.justification: Low mass loss and inhibited oxygen release are mechanistically consistent; this statement is supported.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect (target)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows LFP has higher onset and lower peak temperatures but does not state LFP is immune to thermal runaway under all conditions; this is an unsupported universal claim.

option_judgments.label: Correct (not incorrect)
option_judgments.justification: The structural difference (olivine vs layered oxide) is explicitly cited as the cause of LFP's superior thermal stability, supporting the onset temperature gap.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect (target)
option_judgments.justification: Lower cathode reaction onset under high-temperature shock does not guarantee earlier ignition under all abuse modes; universalizing a conditional finding is unsupported.

option_judgments.label: Correct (not incorrect)
option_judgments.justification: Higher decomposition enthalpy and inhibited oxygen release from P-O bonds are directly stated as LFP's thermal advantage mechanism.

option_judgments.label: Correct (not incorrect)
option_judgments.justification: Greater oxygen availability from NCM523 decomposition is mechanistically consistent with higher combustion mass loss; this is a supported inference.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing conditionally supported comparative claims from unsupported universal generalizations about LFP vs NCM523 thermal behavior
analysis.primary_boundary: Comparative thermal data is from high-temperature shock tests only; universalization to all abuse modes is not licensed
disallowed_extrapolations: LFP immunity to thermal runaway under all conditions
NCM523 always ignites first regardless of abuse mode

solver_view.stem: NCM523 batteries at varying SOC levels are exposed to high-temperature shock. Peak heat release rates (HRR) at 100%, 70%, 50%, 25%, and 0% SOC are 165.3, 108.9, 69.1, 32.2, and 7.0 kW respectively. Additionally, decreasing SOC requires higher surface temperatures to ignite internal cells, implying longer shock exposure. In a separate distance experiment with LFP batteries, ignition time t1 follows y = 0.37x + 5.3 (x = distance in cm, y = t1 in seconds) for 15–60 cm range. Which TWO statements are supported by the evidence?
options: A. Reducing NCM523 SOC from 100% to 0% decreases peak HRR by more than 95%, indicating SOC has a dominant effect on heat release magnitude.
B. The linear t1–distance relationship for LFP batteries can be reliably extrapolated to distances beyond 60 cm using the same equation.
C. A lower-SOC NCM523 battery requires a longer high-temperature shock exposure to reach ignition, but once ignited, releases substantially less peak heat than a higher-SOC cell.
D. The t1–distance linear fit implies that at 0 cm distance, ignition would occur at approximately 5.3 seconds, and this value is valid for contact ignition scenarios.
E. SOC affects both the energy available for heat release and the threshold surface temperature needed for ignition in NCM523 batteries under high-temperature shock.
F. Because LFP peak HRR decreases with increasing distance, LFP batteries at 60 cm are safer than NCM523 batteries at 100% SOC and 15 cm distance under all fire scenarios.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: (165.3 - 7.0)/165.3 = 95.8% reduction; the >95% claim is numerically supported by the reported HRR values.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The linear relationship is explicitly bounded to 15–60 cm; extrapolation beyond 60 cm is outside the stated validity range.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Lower SOC requires higher surface temperature for ignition (longer exposure) and produces lower peak HRR; both are directly supported.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The equation is valid only for 15–60 cm; applying it at x=0 is outside the fitted range and the context is LFP distance tests, not contact ignition.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: SOC affects peak HRR (energy release) and the surface temperature required for ignition; both effects are directly reported for NCM523.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Cross-chemistry safety comparison under 'all fire scenarios' is not supported; evidence covers only high-temperature shock conditions.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Applying SOC-dependent HRR and ignition threshold data alongside distance-dependent ignition time bounds to evaluate multi-condition claims
analysis.primary_boundary: Linear t1–distance fit valid only for 15–60 cm; SOC effects reported only for NCM523 under high-temperature shock
disallowed_extrapolations: Extrapolating t1 equation beyond 60 cm
Cross-chemistry safety ranking under all fire scenarios

solver_view.prompt: A safety engineer is evaluating whether a BiLSTM-based thermal runaway prediction model, trained on NCM523 and LFP pouch cell data under high-temperature shock conditions, can be deployed for real-time TR prediction in a new battery pack using a different cathode chemistry and cell format. The model achieved MAPE below 4% across all six tested prediction conditions on virtual simulation data, but prediction accuracy dropped significantly when applied to experimental temperature data due to limited experimental data availability. Identify TWO distinct limitations that constrain the direct deployment of this model to the new application, and explain the engineering reasoning behind each limitation.

ground_truth.gold_answer: First limitation: The model requires re-parameterization and updated boundary conditions for any chemistry, cell form, or operating condition outside the tested NCM523 and LFP pouch cell scope. Applying it directly to a different cathode chemistry and cell format without re-parameterization constitutes an unsupported transfer, because thermal runaway thresholds, reaction onset temperatures, and heat release characteristics are chemistry- and geometry-specific. Second limitation: The model's accuracy degrades substantially when predicting real experimental temperatures compared to virtual simulation data, due to limited experimental training data. In a real deployment scenario, the model would operate on experimental sensor data, placing it in the regime where accuracy is known to be significantly reduced, making the sub-4% MAPE figure from simulation conditions an overestimate of real-world performance.
must_include: re-parameterization required for different chemistry or cell format
accuracy degradation from simulation to experimental data due to limited experimental data
MAPE below 4% applies to studied conditions only and does not generalize directly
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming the model is entirely unusable without stating the specific boundary conditions
Asserting the model would fail catastrophically without evidence of failure mode severity
Generalizing the 4% MAPE to experimental or out-of-distribution conditions

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Identifying scope-of-validity limitations of a data-driven TR prediction model and connecting them to engineering deployment constraints
analysis.primary_boundary: Model validated only on NCM523 and LFP pouch cells under high-temperature shock; accuracy figures apply to simulation data conditions

solver_view.stem: A LiCoO2/C battery is subjected to a 150°C oven exposure test. Measurements show SEI layer decomposition beginning near 70°C and the anode/electrolyte reaction initiating near 120°C. Which of the following statements about the subsequent thermal runaway sequence are supported by these observations?
options: A. SEI decomposition alone generates sufficient heat to directly trigger electrolyte and cathode decomposition without requiring the anode/electrolyte reaction.
B. The anode/electrolyte reaction generates enough heat to further trigger electrolyte and cathode decomposition reactions.
C. Electrolyte and cathode decomposition release heat and gas that can cause explosion.
D. The anode/electrolyte reaction onset temperature in this test is consistent with the general threshold of intercalated lithium reacting with electrolyte above 120°C.
E. SEI decomposition in this test begins at a temperature below the general 90–120°C threshold range, indicating this threshold is universally inapplicable.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence attributes the triggering of electrolyte/cathode decomposition to the anode/electrolyte reaction, not to SEI decomposition alone.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states the anode/electrolyte reaction churns out enough heat to trigger further electrolyte and cathode decomposition.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states electrolyte and cathode decomposition give off heat and gas and can cause explosion.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: General threshold for intercalated lithium reacting with electrolyte is >120°C; the test shows onset near 120°C, which is consistent.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The 70°C onset is a single-test observation; inferring universal inapplicability of the 90–120°C range is an unsupported boundary overreach.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-step causal chain of thermal runaway stages and consistency of test observations with general thresholds
analysis.primary_boundary: Single LiCoO2/C oven-test observation; general thresholds are separate claims and cannot be universally invalidated by one test
disallowed_extrapolations: Concluding SEI decomposition alone drives full thermal runaway
Declaring general thresholds universally invalid from one test result

solver_view.stem: In a nail penetration-induced thermal runaway test of a large-format LIB pack, the intra-cell temperature difference reached 791.8°C, heat transfer via electrode connectors was 1/10 that via the shell, and onset temperature was lower than in an oven-heat-induced test. A thermal management engineer is assessing heat propagation pathways and detection feasibility. Which statements are supported?
options: A. The dominant inter-component heat transfer pathway during nail penetration thermal runaway is through the electrode connectors rather than the shell.
B. Surface temperature sensors are likely to underestimate core temperatures during thermal runaway, impairing early detection.
C. The large intra-cell temperature gradient confirms that a single surface measurement point is sufficient for accurate core thermal state estimation during thermal runaway.
D. Nail penetration produces a lower onset temperature for thermal runaway than oven heating under the test conditions reported.
E. The primary determinant of nail penetration thermal runaway severity is the total Joule heat generated by the internal short-circuit current.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states connector heat transfer is only 1/10 of shell heat transfer, making the shell the dominant pathway, not connectors.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states significant core-surface temperature discrepancy during thermal runaway impairs early-stage detection via surface sensors.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: A 791.8°C intra-cell gradient directly contradicts the sufficiency of a single surface point; large gradients make surface readings unreliable.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states nail penetration test offered lower onset temperature compared with oven-heat-induced thermal runaway.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states thermal runaway from nail penetration was primarily determined by total Joule heat from the ISC current.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Heat transfer pathway dominance, detection limitations, onset temperature comparison, and primary severity determinant in nail penetration thermal runaway
analysis.primary_boundary: Large-format LIB pack nail penetration test; findings are not generalized to all cell formats or abuse modes
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing connector vs. shell dominance to all cell geometries
Concluding surface sensors are always adequate

solver_view.stem: A battery module designer is comparing separator choices and cooling strategies. Evidence shows: ISC-induced thermal runaway mechanism shifts with separator melting point; direct-contact liquid cooling achieves 1.5–3× air cooling performance; indirect-contact liquid achieves >3× air cooling but at efficiency cost; PCC material between cells reduced surrounding-cell peak temperature by 60°C. Which conclusions are directly supported?
options: A. Using a high-melting-point separator shifts the dominant ISC-induced thermal runaway mechanism from separator melt-down to electrode decomposition.
B. Indirect-contact liquid cooling always outperforms direct-contact liquid cooling in both heat transfer rate and system efficiency.
C. Direct-contact liquid cooling provides a heat transfer improvement of 1.5–3× relative to air cooling.
D. PCC material between cells can reduce the maximum temperature of adjacent cells by 60°C, indicating it hinders thermal runaway propagation.
E. Reciprocating air flow can reduce pack temperature difference by 4°C (72%) within 120 seconds, making it equivalent in absolute cooling capacity to indirect-contact liquid cooling.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states high-melting-point separators shift ISC thermal runaway cause to electrode decomposition rather than separator melt-down.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states indirect-contact achieves >3× only at efficiency cost; it does not claim indirect-contact outperforms direct-contact in efficiency.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly states direct-contact liquids achieve 1.5–3× higher cooling performance than air cooling.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states PCC between cells decreased surrounding cell maximum temperature by 60°C, indicating effective hindrance of propagation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence reports reciprocating air flow reduces temperature difference by 72% in 120s but makes no equivalence claim to liquid cooling absolute capacity.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Separator-dependent ISC mechanism shift and comparative cooling strategy performance boundaries
analysis.primary_boundary: Cooling comparisons are relative to air cooling baselines; no cross-method absolute equivalence is supported
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming indirect-contact is superior in all dimensions
Equating reciprocating air flow to liquid cooling absolute capacity

solver_view.prompt: During nail penetration-induced thermal runaway of a large-format LIB pack, an intra-cell temperature difference of 791.8°C was recorded, and heat transfer via electrode connectors was measured at 1/10 that via the cell shell. A safety engineer proposes using a single surface-mounted thermocouple at the connector terminal to monitor core thermal state and detect early-stage thermal runaway. Identify two distinct limitations of this approach, explaining the mechanism behind each limitation based on the experimental evidence described.

ground_truth.gold_answer: First limitation: The dominant heat transfer pathway during nail penetration thermal runaway is through the cell shell, not the electrode connectors (connector pathway carries only 1/10 the heat of the shell). A sensor placed at the connector terminal therefore monitors a thermally minor pathway and will underread the actual heat flux driving thermal runaway propagation. Second limitation: The 791.8°C intra-cell temperature gradient confirms a large discrepancy between core and surface temperatures during thermal runaway. Surface-mounted sensors systematically underestimate core temperatures, which impairs early-stage detection because the dangerous thermal event at the core is not reflected promptly or accurately at the surface measurement point.
must_include: connector heat transfer is 1/10 of shell heat transfer, making connector location thermally unrepresentative
large core-to-surface temperature discrepancy (up to 791.8°C intra-cell gradient) impairs early-stage detection via surface sensors
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming this limitation applies identically to all cell formats or all abuse conditions beyond the large-format nail penetration test described
Quantifying a specific detection delay time not supported by the evidence
Asserting that no surface sensor configuration could ever provide useful thermal information

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Integration of heat transfer pathway dominance and core-surface temperature discrepancy to evaluate sensor placement adequacy
analysis.primary_boundary: Large-format LIB pack nail penetration test; connector vs. shell ratio and intra-cell gradient are specific to this configuration
